title: Quiz: Scalability Analysis
description: Test your understanding of quantum computer scaling challenges, overhead calculations, and modular architectures

questions.question: For surface code error correction with code distance d, how does the number of physical qubits per logical qubit scale?

options.text: Linearly with d: O(d)
Quadratically with d: O(d^2)
Cubically with d: O(d^3)
Exponentially with d: O(2^d)

questions.explanation: The surface code requires O(d^2) physical qubits per logical qubit, where d is the code distance. A distance-d surface code uses a d x d lattice of data qubits plus nearly as many ancilla qubits for syndrome measurement, giving roughly 2d^2 total physical qubits per logical qubit.

questions.question: What is the primary scaling bottleneck unique to superconducting quantum computers?

options.text: Atom loss during computation
Wiring and control line routing to each qubit
Motional mode heating in ion chains
Probabilistic two-qubit gate success

questions.explanation: Superconducting qubits require multiple control and readout lines per qubit (typically 3-5), all of which must pass through the dilution refrigerator. This 'wiring problem' is a key bottleneck as qubit counts increase to thousands. Solutions include multiplexed readout, 3D integration, and on-chip cryo-CMOS control.

questions.question: In modular quantum computing, why are photonic interconnects advantageous for linking quantum processing units?

options.text: Photons can operate at room temperature and travel long distances with low loss
Photonic links are faster than any other interconnect technology
Photons automatically error-correct during transmission
Photonic interconnects don't require any calibration

questions.explanation: Photonic interconnects are advantageous because photons can travel through optical fibers at room temperature over long distances (meters to kilometers) with relatively low loss. This allows quantum modules to be physically separated, simplifying thermal management and enabling flexible architectures.

questions.question: For a fault-tolerant quantum computer running Shor's algorithm to break RSA-2048, approximately how many physical qubits are estimated to be needed?

options.text: ~10,000 physical qubits
~100,000 physical qubits
~10-20 million physical qubits
~1 billion physical qubits

questions.explanation: Breaking RSA-2048 requires approximately 4,000-6,000 logical qubits. With current surface code overhead estimates (1,000-10,000 physical qubits per logical qubit depending on error rates), this translates to roughly 10-20 million physical qubits. This is why cryptographically-relevant quantum computing is still years away.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of neutral atom quantum computers for scaling? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Optical tweezer arrays can naturally address thousands of atoms
No individual wiring required per qubit - global laser addressing
Qubits can be physically rearranged for optimal connectivity
Gate operations are faster than any other platform

questions.explanation: Neutral atom systems have several scaling advantages: (a) optical tweezer arrays can trap thousands of atoms using spatial light modulators, (b) qubits are addressed by global laser beams rather than individual wires, and (c) atoms can be physically moved to create any desired connectivity graph. However, gate speeds are not the fastest - superconducting qubits have faster gates.

questions.question: The 'effective qubits' metric accounts for both qubit count and quality. If a system has 1000 physical qubits with 99% two-qubit fidelity, and another has 100 qubits with 99.9% fidelity, which likely has more effective qubits for deep circuits?

options.text: The 1000-qubit system, because it has 10x more qubits
The 100-qubit system, because higher fidelity enables deeper circuits
They are equivalent in effective qubits
Cannot be determined without knowing the specific algorithm

questions.explanation: For deep circuits, fidelity matters more than raw qubit count. With 99% fidelity, a circuit of depth 50 would have overall fidelity ~0.99^50 = 0.61. With 99.9% fidelity, depth 500 gives ~0.999^500 = 0.61. The higher-fidelity system can run circuits ~10x deeper, making 100 high-fidelity qubits often more effective than 1000 lower-fidelity qubits.

questions.question: What is a QCCD (Quantum Charge-Coupled Device) architecture primarily designed to address?

options.text: The wiring problem in superconducting qubits
The ion chain length limitation in trapped ion systems
The probabilistic gate problem in photonic systems
The atom loss problem in neutral atom systems

questions.explanation: QCCD architecture addresses the limitation that trapped ion chains become difficult to control beyond ~50 ions. QCCD uses segmented traps where small groups of ions can be shuttled between zones, enabling larger systems through multiple interaction zones connected by ion transport rather than a single long chain.

questions.question: Cryo-CMOS (CMOS electronics operating at cryogenic temperatures) could eventually eliminate the need for room-temperature control electronics in superconducting quantum computers.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. Cryo-CMOS aims to place control electronics at cryogenic temperatures (typically 4K) close to the qubits, potentially eliminating the wiring bottleneck for large-scale systems. While power dissipation limits currently restrict cryo-CMOS to ~1000 qubits, ongoing research is pushing these boundaries. Full integration could significantly simplify scaling.
